scenario: Redmine work session - audit the Issue contract before a teardown rework

map_quality.rationale: The answer correctly identifies Redmine as a Rails issue-tracker, maps the directory layout (app/, lib/redmine/, lib/plugins/, test/), names Issue as the central record with its file, and gives file:line for the three shared mixin entry points (attachable, watchable, customizable). However, it omits the stored collaborator records themselves — app/models/attachment.rb, app/models/watcher.rb, app/models/custom_value.rb — which the rubric names as required map items; an agent still needs a lookup to find them.

specificity.rationale: The mixin paths are concrete and include line numbers (e.g. `lib/plugins/acts_as_attachable/lib/acts_as_attachable.rb:52`), but the rubric explicitly requires app/models/attachment.rb, app/models/watcher.rb, and app/models/custom_value.rb, which are entirely absent from the answer.

justification.rationale: The answer lists the acts_as plugin names but never explains WHY the architecture uses this plugin pattern — it does not state that shared behaviors are factored into reusable mixins so many record types can opt in without duplicating code; it only names the pieces.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty flags anywhere; the answer presents all connections as confirmed even though the polymorphic association wiring (how each mixin actually hangs the collaborator row off the host record) is left unstated and unverified.

relationship.rationale: The answer names the mixin files but states no connecting relationship: it never says that Issue opts into each behavior via the shared mixin, that the collaborator row hangs off the host through a polymorphic association (e.g. has_many :attachments, as: :container), or what the polymorphic join key is — exactly the listing-without-relations pattern the rubric penalizes.

steps.step: Map the Issue contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: All three contracts are mapped with file:line (acts_as_attachable issue.rb:48/acts_as_attachable.rb:59, acts_as_customizable issue.rb:49/acts_as_customizable.rb:33,36,42, acts_as_watchable issue.rb:50/acts_as_watchable.rb:33), plus the teardown layering explained in the final paragraph; the agent can pick an entry point and work without re-exploring.

specificity.rationale: Line numbers are present throughout, but mixin files are cited as bare names (acts_as_attachable.rb, acts_as_customizable.rb, acts_as_watchable.rb) without the lib/plugins/ path the rubric asks for; the agent will need one targeted lookup per mixin to resolve the file location.

justification.rationale: The answer explains practical implications well (delete_all skips callbacks, three-layer teardown) but never states WHY the architecture uses shared mixins reused across many models — the one-mixin-one-polymorphic-record design rationale is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere; the answer presents every mixin method and association as confirmed, despite not having opened the polymorphic as: qualifiers or verified the full mixin internals.

relationship.rationale: The mixin-to-has_many path is stated for all three contracts (acts_as_attachable → has_many :attachments dependent: :destroy, etc.), but the polymorphic as: qualifier (as: :container / :watchable / :customized) that binds the collaborator record to the host model is never named, leaving a key part of the relation unstated.

steps.step: Trace how an issue and its attached data are handled on change

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces create/update and destroy with file:line throughout and clearly marks inline vs async (deliver_later, after_commit, perform_later). The destroy teardown path is mapped (attachments :destroy, journals :destroy, time_entries :destroy, relations/watchers/custom_values/reactions :delete_all at issue.rb:260 and project.rb:40), but the mixin wiring — how the has_many associations arrive on Issue in the first place — is absent, so the agent cannot follow the chain all the way back to origin.

specificity.rationale: Extremely concrete throughout: issue.rb:117-134, :246, :260, :875, :1836, :1856, :1863, :1948, :2000, :2009, :2029, :2041, :2049; journal.rb:66; mailer.rb:108, :141, :147; webhook.rb:114-115; issue_nested_set.rb:30; destroy_project_job.rb:48; project.rb:40. The :destroy vs :delete_all distinction per dependent type is named.

justification.rationale: The answer explains what each callback does but not WHY teardown is routed through dependent associations on a shared mixin (so every participant inherits the same cleanup without repeating the logic). The architectural rationale behind the mixin pattern is never stated.

uncertainty.rationale: No explicit flags where cleanup semantics were not fully verified (e.g., whether :delete_all on relations bypasses callbacks, or whether the mixin's has_many was read directly). The agent is left to assume the stated dependent: values are correct.

relationship.rationale: The destroy chain is partially assembled (before_destroy → dependent teardown → after_destroy :update_parent_attributes → after_commit journal), and the create/update callback sequence is ordered. However the answer never writes the connecting path from the shared mixin through the polymorphic has_many to the attached row destruction, so the first link in the chain (mixin wiring → has_many :attachments, as: :container, dependent: :destroy) is missing.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Issue contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer gives a broad, file-line-rich audit of everything that directly references the Issue model, but misses what the rubric specifically demands: enumerating for EACH of the three shared contracts (attachable/watchable/customizable) the full fan-out of participating models, including those such as Message, News, WikiPage, Board, Document, and Group that opt into the mixin and never name the collaborator class. The three-contract participant map is the 'core of the ticket' per the rubric, and it is absent.

specificity.rationale: For the broad Issue-dependent audit the answer does provide concrete file:line citations throughout (e.g., `changeset.rb:24,162`, `version.rb:127`, `mail_handler.rb:190,201,232`), but the mixin-participant models the rubric singles out (Message, Board, WikiPage, etc.) are not cited at all, so specificity is strong only within the wrong scope.

justification.rationale: The answer explains how each file uses Issue directly (e.g., 'scan_for_issues auto-links'), but never explains WHY a model is a contract participant, namely that it opts into a shared mixin which hangs the collaborator record off it through a polymorphic association. The architectural rationale the rubric asks for is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The rubric specifically asks the answer to flag that a text search for the collaborator class misses participants that reach it only through the mixin's polymorphic association; the answer raises no such caveat anywhere, leaving the agent blind to the gap.

relationship.rationale: The connections stated are direct model associations (belongs_to :issue, has_many :issues, HABTM), not the shared-mixin-to-polymorphic-association path (acts_as_attachable -> has_many :attachments, as: :container) that links each contract participant to the collaborator record; the rubric's central relationship requirement is unmet for the three contracts.

steps.step: Trace how issue identity and visibility are guarded

map_quality.rationale: Comprehensive file:line coverage of the full visibility stack: predicate definitions at issue.rb:137/172/84, every cross-subsystem caller (autocomplete, wiki macro, mail handler, reports, gantt, calendar, journals, relations, time entries, reactions, version) with exact lines. An agent could trace any entry point without re-reading the codebase, though the specific guard paths for the three shared-contract attached rows (Attachment, Watcher, CustomValue) are not mapped as a group.

specificity.rationale: Guards are named concretely: `visible_condition`, `visible?`, `scope :visible`, permission keys (`view_issues`, `issues_visibility`, `is_private`), all with file:line; no vague phrases like 'the permission code'.

justification.rationale: States the invariant ('no trusted handoff of a bare issue id, re-checked at every subsystem boundary') but does not explain WHY attached rows inherit the host's visibility by design (e.g., why Reaction delegates to `object.visible?(user)` or why TimeEntry bakes in `Issue.visible_condition`); the design rationale is asserted, not derived.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the full map with no flags for implicit or delegated guards the agent should verify before relying on; a system this large (16+ call sites) almost certainly has edge cases the agent should confirm, but none are surfaced.

relationship.rationale: Several paths are stated explicitly: `Journal.visible` joins `Issue.visible_condition` (journal.rb:72), `TimeEntry.visible_condition` joins through the issue (time_entry.rb:68-70), `Reaction.visible?` delegates to `object.visible?` (reaction.rb:26); but the three core contract paths (Attachment→host, Watcher→host, CustomValue→host) are never assembled as a chain, so the attachable/watchable/customizable leg of the relationship is missing.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps Issue's own teardown chain thoroughly (dependent: declarations, nested_set, journals, time_entries, relations) with file:line and a risk ranking, but the rubric asks for blast radius across ALL THREE shared contracts and their full participant sets — message.rb, news.rb, document.rb, wiki_page.rb, board.rb, project.rb etc. that also opt into acts_as_attachable/watchable/customizable — and those models are entirely absent, leaving the agent with a large unverified tail.

specificity.rationale: For every item the answer does name, file:line citations are present and concrete (app/models/issue.rb:41-46, acts_as_customizable.rb:36,42, app/jobs/destroy_project_job.rb:48); the gap is that the omitted participant fan-out (other attachable/watchable/customizable models) carries no specificity at all.

justification.rationale: Risk levels are explained with mechanism reasoning: 'delete_all skips relation_removed', 'attachment files on disk leak if teardown order changes', 'any teardown reorder can deadlock or orphan subtrees' — these are actionable rationales; the missing participants simply have no justification because they are not named.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer uses 're-verify' as a soft hedge on lower-risk items but never explicitly flags which participants it could not confirm or what a grep-based search would miss (models that reach the collaborator only through the mixin); the agent is left unwarned about the coverage gap.

relationship.rationale: The three mixin files are named (acts_as_customizable.rb, acts_as_attachable.rb, acts_as_watchable.rb) but no explicit path is assembled from 'storage/teardown change → shared mixin → each affected participant'; the answer lists files with risk labels rather than tracing how the change propagates through the polymorphic association to every model that opted in, and the other 10+ participant models never appear as endpoints of any path.

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a complete, actable map: edit targets with file:line (issue.rb:41-46, acts_as_attachable.rb:59, acts_as_watchable.rb:33, acts_as_customizable.rb:32-42, reaction.rb:39, issue_nested_set.rb:30), participating models grouped by area with file:line, tests_to_update listing 19 specific test files, fixtures_to_update with exact names, and a behavioral spec for the new destroy-teardown test. A teammate could land the change from this alone. Minor gap: review/touch grouping is by functional area (request_handling, models_and_associations) rather than by contract (attachable/watchable/customizable), making it slightly harder to audit one contract at a time.

specificity.rationale: File:line is present throughout — edit targets (app/models/issue.rb:41-46,124-134,260,1856-1914,1948-1986,2049-2088), test files (test/unit/issue_test.rb, test/functional/issues_controller_test.rb), fixtures (test/fixtures/issues.yml, journals.yml) and mixin defs (lib/plugins/acts_as_attachable/lib/acts_as_attachable.rb:59) are all named exactly; nothing is left at the level of 'the model layer' or 'add tests'.

justification.rationale: The teardown_contract section gives modes (dependent: :destroy vs delete_all) and the blast_radius_ranked section gives risk labels with brief reasons, and the shared_behaviors block states each mixin's teardown mechanism. However, the review/touch lists (controllers, models, reporting) do not consistently explain which of the three contracts (attachable/watchable/customizable) makes each file relevant to this change, so a teammate cannot tell at a glance whether journal.rb or timelog_controller.rb is in the map for attachment, watcher, or custom-value reasons.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer is consistently confident throughout — it does not flag the HABTM join-row cleanup semantics as worth verifying, does not note that delete_all-mode teardowns skip callbacks and need manual confirmation, and does not call out any participant reached only through a mixin as uncertain. The blast_radius_ranked list assigns risk levels but that is a judgment, not an uncertainty flag asking the agent to verify before committing.

relationship.rationale: The shared_behaviors and teardown_contract blocks do name the relation chain explicitly (e.g. 'has_many :attachments dependent: :destroy (acts_as_attachable.rb:59)', 'watchers delete_all (acts_as_watchable.rb:33)'). However, the large participant lists (models_and_associations, request_handling) name endpoints without stating which shared mixin / polymorphic association makes each participant part of this edit; tests_to_update similarly list files without mapping each test to the contract it exercises, so the chain from storage/teardown change through mixin to participant is explicit only in the shared-behaviors section, not across the rest of the map.
